fitting models

You are:

reasoning about data

evaluating evidence

making disciplined claims

This is the difference between:

tool usage
and

analytical thinking
crumbs: 14  From Foundations to Real-World Practice

title: From Foundations to Real-World Practice
text:
Beyond Analysis
In real-world environments, analysis is only one part of the system.
Models are often expected to:

run automatically

serve predictions to users

integrate with applications

operate in changing environments

This introduces a new layer:

machine learning systems
crumbs: 14  From Foundations to Real-World Practice

title: From Foundations to Real-World Practice
text:
From Model to System
So far, you have worked with:

datasets

notebooks / Quarto

local execution

In practice, models need to be:

saved and versioned

exposed through interfaces

deployed to servers or cloud environments

monitored over time

This is where data science becomes engineering.
crumbs: 14  From Foundations to Real-World Practice

title: From Foundations to Real-World Practice
text:
What Comes Next
The next stage of CDI focuses on:

packaging trained models

building APIs for predictions

deploying models into production environments

monitoring performance and data drift

updating models as data evolves

This is often referred to as:

ML → Deployment → DevOps
crumbs: 14  From Foundations to Real-World Practice

title: From Foundations to Real-World Practice
text:
Important Distinction
This guide focused on:

how to think, analyze, and interpret correctly

The next stage focuses on:

how to build, deploy, and maintain systems

Both are important.
But they require different skills and different ways of thinking.
crumbs: 14  From Foundations to Real-World Practice

title: From Foundations to Real-World Practice
text:
CDI Insight

Strong systems built on weak reasoning fail quietly.
Strong reasoning is the foundation of reliable systems.
crumbs: 14  From Foundations to Real-World Practice

title: From Foundations to Real-World Practice
text:
How to Move Forward
When transitioning to real-world practice:
Start by asking:

What problem am I solving?

What data is available?

What level of accuracy is sufficient?

What are the risks of being wrong?

Then extend into:

how predictions will be used

how the system will be maintained

how performance will be monitored
crumbs: 14  From Foundations to Real-World Practice

title: From Foundations to Real-World Practice
text:
Your Position Now
You are now prepared to:

approach real-world datasets with structure

build models with discipline

interpret results responsibly

communicate findings clearly

This is a strong foundation for:

advanced modeling

domain-specific applications

production systems
crumbs: 14  From Foundations to Real-World Practice

title: From Foundations to Real-World Practice
text:
Final Reflection
A model is not the end of analysis.
A result is not the end of reasoning.
A prediction is not the end of decision-making.
The goal is not just to produce outputs.
The goal is to produce:

understanding that can be used responsibly
crumbs: 14  From Foundations to Real-World Practice

title: From Foundations to Real-World Practice
text:
Where to Go Next
→ ML → Deployment → DevOps Track
In the next track, you will learn how to:

turn models into working systems

expose predictions through APIs

deploy models to real environments

monitor and improve systems over time
crumbs: 14  From Foundations to Real-World Practice

title: From Foundations to Real-World Practice
text:
Closing Thought
Data science is not only about building models.
It is about connecting:
data → understanding → action
And doing so in a way that is:

clear

reproducible

responsible
crumbs: 14  From Foundations to Real-World Practice

title: Appendix
text:
Project Structure Overview
This appendix collects technical reference material that supports the Foundations Track.
It documents the project structure, environment setup, rendering workflow, and reusable patterns used throughout the guide.
A standard CDI Quarto-first project looks like this:

title: Appendix
text:
data-science/
├── index.qmd
├── 00-preface.qmd
├── 01-setting-up-environment.qmd
├── ...
├── data/
├── figures/
├── scripts/
│   └── bash/
├── docs/
├── _quarto.yml
└── requirements.txt

Key Directories

data/
stores raw and cleaned datasets
figures/
stores plots that are explicitly saved during rendering
scripts/bash/
contains helper scripts such as setup-env.sh and build.sh
docs/
contains the rendered Quarto site (GitHub Pages output)

title: Appendix
text:
Environment Setup Reference
This project uses a local virtual environment (.venv) for reproducibility.

Create environment manually
python3 -m venv .venv
source .venv/bin/activate
pip install -r requirements.txt


Render the book
quarto render
Or use the helper script:
bash scripts/bash/build.sh

title: Appendix
text:
Quarto Workflow Summary
The workflow for this guide is:
Python code
    ↓
Quarto chapter (.qmd)
    ↓
quarto render
    ↓
docs/ (static site)
All figures and outputs are generated during rendering.

title: Appendix
text:
Reusable Pandas Patterns

Select columns
df[["col1", "col2"]]


Filter rows
df[df["col"] > value]


Group and aggregate
df.groupby("group_col").agg(
    metric=("value_col", "mean")
)


Handle missing values
df["col"] = df["col"].fillna(df["col"].median())


Convert dtype
df["category_col"] = df["category_col"].astype("category")

title: Appendix
text:
Reproducibility Checklist
Before finalizing any chapter:

confirm no missing values remain (unless justified)

confirm no unintended duplicates

confirm correct data types

confirm figures render correctly

rebuild the book (quarto render)

open docs/index.html to verify output

title: Appendix
text:
Data Sources

Iris dataset (Fisher 1936)

title: Appendix
text:
Software and Tools
This guide uses a small set of widely adopted tools for data analysis and visualization:

Python (Python Software Foundation 2024) — general-purpose programming language for data analysis

pandas (McKinney et al. 2010) — data manipulation and tabular data handling

NumPy (Harris et al. 2020) — numerical computing and array operations

matplotlib (Hunter 2007) — foundational plotting library

seaborn (Waskom 2021) — statistical data visualization built on matplotlib

These tools form a standard ecosystem for reproducible data science workflows.

title: Appendix
text:
Closing Note
The goal of this Foundations Track is not just to teach syntax.
It is to teach structure, discipline, and reproducibility.
The same workflow you used here can be applied to:

new datasets

new domains

larger analytical projects

future CDI guides
